q:
Among the two statements

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q is a contradiction and S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q is a tautology
question:
Among the two statements

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q is a contradiction and S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q is a tautology
solution:
Given,

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q

≡~p∨q∧p∧~q as A⇒B≡~A∨B

≡~p∧p∧~q∨q∧p∧~q

≡f∧~q∨f∧p

≡f∨f≡f

 

S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q

≡p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q

≡p∨~p∧q∨p∨~p∧~q

≡t∧q∨t∧~q≡q∨~q≡t

Hence, both statement are true.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $p$ be the statement " $x$ is an irrational number", $q$ be the statement " $y$ is a transcendental number", and $r$ be the statement " $\mathrm{x}$ is a rational number iff $\mathrm{y}$ is a transcendental number".
Statement $-1$ : $r$ is equivalent to either $q$ or $p$
Statement $-2: r$ is equivalent to $\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)$.
question:
Let $p$ be the statement " $x$ is an irrational number", $q$ be the statement " $y$ is a transcendental number", and $r$ be the statement " $\mathrm{x}$ is a rational number iff $\mathrm{y}$ is a transcendental number".
Statement $-1$ : $r$ is equivalent to either $q$ or $p$
Statement $-2: r$ is equivalent to $\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)$.
options: Statement $-1$ is false, Statement $-2$ is true
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true, Statement $-2$ is a correct explanation for Statement $-1$
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is not a correct explanation for Statement $-1$.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is false.
solution:
Given statement $r=\sim p \leftrightarrow q$
Statement $-1: r_1=(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$
Statement $-2: r_2=\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)=(p \wedge q) \vee(\sim q \wedge \sim p)$
From the truth table of $r, r_1$ and $r_2$, $r=r_1$.
Hence Statement $-1$ is true and Statement $-2$ is false.

≡F→T≡T

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $p$ and $q$ be any two logical statements and $\mathrm{r}: \mathrm{p} \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$. If $r$ has a truth value $F$, then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively:
question: Let $p$ and $q$ be any two logical statements and $\mathrm{r}: \mathrm{p} \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$. If $r$ has a truth value $F$, then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively:
solution:
$p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ has truth value $\mathrm{F}$.
It means $p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ is false.
It means $p$ is true and $\sim p \vee q$ is false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and both $\sim p$ and $q$ are false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and $q$ is false.

q:
Consider the following three statements:

A If 3+3=7 then 4+3=8

B If 5+3=8 then earth is flat.

C If both A and B are true then 5+6=17.

Then, which of the following statements is correct?
question:
Consider the following three statements:

A If 3+3=7 then 4+3=8

B If 5+3=8 then earth is flat.

C If both A and B are true then 5+6=17.

Then, which of the following statements is correct?
solution:
Truth table for if-then statement is

p q p→q
T T T
T F F
F T T
F F T

A 3+3=7 (false), and 4+3=8 (false), therefore A is true.

B 5+3=8 (true) and earth is flat (false), hence B is false.

C Since, B is false and 5+6=17 (false), hence C is true.

q: Consider the statement: "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>n</mi></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>41</mn></math> is prime". Then which one of the following is true?
question: Consider the statement: "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>n</mi></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>41</mn></math> is prime". Then which one of the following is true?
solution:
Given Pn=n2-n+41

P3=9-3+41=47

P5=25-5+41=61

Hence P3 and P5 both are prime.

Hence, both are true.

q: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to :
question: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to :
solution: Since truth value of $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ and $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$ are same, hence $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$.

question:

solution:
We have,

S1: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r

For S1:  If p is F, q is T, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

p⇒r≡T

So, p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r≡F

then F⇒F≡F

i.e. S1 is not a tautology

S2: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r

S2:  If p is T, q is F, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

p⇒r∨q⇒r≡T

then p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r≡F

i.e. S2 is not a tautology

subject: Mathematics
q: The contrapositive of the statement ''if I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor" is
question: The contrapositive of the statement ''if I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor" is
options: if I will go to the doctor, then I am not feeling well.
if I am feeling well, then I will not go to the doctor.
if I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
if I will go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
solution:
Statement: If I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor.

Let p: I am not feeling well and q: I will go to the doctor.

The given statement is p→q.

We know that the contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

Contrapositive: If I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
